answer:
\boxed{E}

The man’s serum glucose remains stable after an overnight fast primarily due to **glycogenolysis in the liver**, where stored glycogen is broken down into glucose to maintain blood sugar levels. While gluconeogenesis (option G) also contributes, it is less critical in the early hours of fasting when glycogen reserves are still substantial. Other options (e.g., insulin secretion or gut absorption) would either lower glucose or be irrelevant during fasting.
